pattern: Repeatedly factor the current value by trial division, summing every prime factor with multiplicity, and replace the value with that sum; the value stops changing once it is already prime, so the process always settles there.
hints: To get the sum of prime factors with multiplicity, trial-divide the number by 2, then by every odd number up from 3, dividing out each factor completely (adding it to the sum once per division) before moving to the next candidate.
If after dividing out all factors up to its square root something greater than 1 remains, that remainder is itself a prime factor and must be added to the sum too.

Keep replacing the number with its prime-factor sum and repeat until a value stops changing; that happens once the value is prime (its only prime factor is itself), with one special composite exception where the sum still equals the number.

edge_inputs.description: already prime — the very first value is already the fixed point
a power of two, so every prime factor is 2 repeated
a value that needs several rounds of replacement before settling
the largest allowed input, itself a power of two
the largest prime not exceeding the constraint ceiling — should reduce to itself in zero steps
a highly composite number whose prime-factor sum with multiplicity is far smaller than the number of divisors might suggest
a solution that only counts DISTINCT prime factors (ignoring multiplicity) would undercount here, since 2 divides evenly many times

stress.description: every integer from 2 up to the constraint ceiling, exercising the full replacement chain for each
